text:
TRIGEMINAL AUTONOMIC CEPHALALGIAS: CLUSTER HEADACHE AND RELATED CONDITIONS
Peter J. Goadsby
*
, in
Neurology and Clinical Neuroscience
, 2007
Verapamil
Verapamil
was first reported to be an effective preventive agent for cluster headache by Meyer and Hardenberg.
84
In an open-label trial with
verapamil
at doses of 160 to 720 mg/day in five CCH patients, a reduction in the mean monthly headache frequency was reported in all patients. In another open-label trial with
verapamil
at doses of 240 to 600 mg/day for ECH and 120 to 1200 mg/day for CCH, an improvement of more than 75% was noted in 33 (69%) of 48 patients.
85
A double-blind, crossover trial in which
verapamil
, 360 mg/day, was compared with the then standard prophylactic drug, lithium, 900 mg/day, each given for 8 weeks, demonstrated equivalent effects in the 24 CCH patients who completed the trial.
86
Verapamil
and lithium were superior to placebo.
Verapamil
caused fewer side effects and had a shorter latency period. In a double-blind, placebo-controlled trial, the efficacy of
verapamil
, 360 mg/day over a 2-week period, was evaluated in 26 patients with ECH.
87
A statistically significant reduction in headache frequency and analgesic consumption occurred in the
verapamil
-treated patients, with a greater reduction in the second week of treatment.
Verapamil
is the preventive drug of choice for both CCH and ECH, the latter when the bout is sufficiently long to establish a suitable dose. Clinical experience has clearly demonstrated that higher doses are needed for cluster headache than for cardiological indications. Dosages commonly employed range from 240 to 960 mg/day in divided doses.
Verapamil
can cause heart block by slowing conduction in the atrioventricular node,
88
as demonstrated by prolongation of the A-H interval.
89
In observing for P-R interval prolongation on the electrocardiogram, clinicians can monitor potential development of heart block, although it is a coarse measure. No formal guidelines are available. After obtaining a baseline electrocardiogram, the author and colleagues start patients on 80 mg three times daily, and the total daily dose is increased in increments of 80 mg every 10 to 14 days. An electrocardiogram is obtained before each increment and at least 10 days after the last dose change. The dosage is increased until the cluster attacks are suppressed, side effects intervene, or the maximum dose of 960 mg/day is achieved. It is unproved clinical experience that standard preparations of
verapamil
are more effective than the modified-release formulations.
58,
90
Constipation is the most common side effect, but dizziness, ankle swelling, nausea, fatigue, hypotension, and bradycardia may also occur. Gingival enlargement is a recognized problem that needs prompt attention from a dentist.
91
β Blockers should not be given concurrently.

Hi guys it’s Jason. I am chronic for the last 2 years with as little as 1 attack daily and as high as 8 DAILY at its worst. After 17 bust attempts I have been pain free and attack free  for a little over month now  with completing my first maintenance bust a few days ago!  I finally found the right dose of 1.75 grams of B+. I have officially been off of all meds since oct.24th the week before my first bust which included topamax, lithium, and I have only had 2 use a needle only twice since then and have mostly aborted all attacks with Red Bull and O2. Even though I didn’t find instant relief i immediately found the attacks lessened in severity once I started busting. I also have changed my diet on a strict low histamine diet and i do the d3 regimen daily, will take 1-3 Benadryl daily as needed (mostly 50 mg at night ) and I take a Zyrtec daily. I only have small level 3 shadows occasionally which I control with Benadryl and ginger.  I even accidentally was given a sip of alcohol (i quit drinking once diagnosed 2 years ago once i saw it triggered attacks) last week my drink was mixed by the waiter at a restaurant  And I waited in terror waiting for the beast to return and It never came back. I also eliminated stress which I think has helped.  It’s crazy because as much as clusters have taken from me and from all of us it has given me something in a sense where now i appreciate these days. I still am very paranoid, certainty have ptsd, and honestly the low histamine diet is tough, but I would rather eat less and then have an attack and I believe histamines and allergies are a direct link to these things....... I also believe the D3 regimen has been instrumental and once I started following it more strictly I have seen GREATER results. I hope and pray I can go another month pain free and have scheduled my next maintenance bust for APRIL 1st. It took me 17 bust every 5 days to get here so any one who is ready to give up DONT! And thank you all for being cluster warriors, and for this site because it has saved my life.

I've been wanting to post on this for a while now...This is probably going to be a long complicated post, most likely MORE than one post...but here goes
Harmala Alkaloids are substances found in varying degrees in plants like Syrian Rue, Banisteriopsis caapi vine, Passionflower and Tobacco (I'm sure there are more)Â  The two with the highest known amounts are the Syrian Rue seeds and the Banisteriopsis Caapi vines.
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmala_alkaloid
The major alkaloids involved are Harmine, Harmaline, and Tetrahydroharmine.Â  These are all Beta Carbolines, They all inhibit the MAO A enzyme, and they all are considered RIMA's--Reversible Inhibitors of MAO subtype A.Â (For more info on RIMA's you can look into the pharmaceutical anti-depressant moclobomide, which has MAO A enzyme as it's mechanism of action.)Â  Â All 3 substances are completely legal in the U.S., as are both the Caapi Vine and Rue seeds.Â  I know I have read that they are hitting some of the same receptors as Psilocybin and LSA, but right now I can't find my sources.
But first we'll touch on MAO inhibitors in general.Â  Everything you hear about them sounds scary, everything seems to say, "Do Not Mix With MAO inhibitors"Â  We need to know that the substances contained in Caapi vines and Syrian Rue seeds are very different drugs than the old style pharmaceutical MAO inhibitors that were usually used for depression.Â  These older MAO inhibitors would inhibit TWO enzymes, MAO A and MAO B, where as the RIMA's only inhibit ONE enzyme, MAO A.Â  This is important because that means you still have an active MAO B enzyme, which makes your body perfectly able to still metabolize Tyramine.Â  With the older MAO inhibitors that inhibited BOTH enzymes you are not able to metabolize tyramine, this can lead to hypertensive crisis, and this is the biggest warning you will hear about with MAO inhibiting drugs.Â  Just remember--THIS IS NOT AN ISSUE WITH THE NATURAL MAO A INHIBITING DRUGS LIKE RUE OR CAAPI VINES.Â  The biggest thing that made me more comfortable with these drugs was when I realized that I have been taking them all my life without knowing it.Â  Certain foods, like chocolate, have this effect naturally andÂ  you'll never notice it.Â  Â Â In fact, Terence Mckenna claims that ALL tryptamines are RIMA's--this means the mushrooms, LSD, and LSA containing seeds that we are taking are all having this effect on us.Â  I can confirm this effect from the experience of smoking DMT while on mushrooms.Â  (More on that later)
So no risk of "hypertensive crisis"Â  Just forget about it.Â  There is the theory that if you are on RIMA's and eat lots and lots of tyramine containing foods that eventually you could "overwhelm" the system and cause this crisis, but I have NEVER heard of this actually happening.Â  From what I've read it seems more like people have been scared of MAO inhibitors for a long time and are reluctant to let go of that fear.Â  One of the biggest sources of tyramine is chees

Hey folks!
First let me say that I'm grateful to find these forums, and the resources in the relevant stickied topics/links already taught me plenty I didn't know about this horrible thing, and some hope to be had in a moment I really, really need.
I thought I'd share my history with CH, since even those who empathize and care for me don't really understand what it's like. I was one of those people that almost never got headaches naturally, and even when I was hungover it was not that common, unless I had a LOT to drink, which predictably became less and less common as I aged. I'm 29 years old and I live in Brazil, and in 2017 I got my first episode of CH, and of course I had no idea what that was, it lasted a week, and came at 3h30 in the morning and went away exactly an hour later, like the world's most brutalizing clockwork. After three days I was absolutely desperate, as anything I'd take had no effect whatsoever, before or during the attack, but I couldn't get an appointment at a neurologist covered by my insurance plan until a week later, so I endured the agony. Fortunately, he did know what CH was, which after a cursory reading of the experiences on this forum seems rather uncommon, but unfortunately he didn't know how to treat it properly. He prescribed Naratriptan each day before bed, and intranasal oxygen at a hospital, but I didn't have time to practice any of it, as it had gone away and I hoped it would never return.
7 months later, in February of 2018, came the second episode, exactly like the first one. The Naratriptan did exactly nothing, and as I lived alone, not in a million years would I be able to get to a hospital by myself, much less in time to be treated for something that lasted an hour. Fuck, I couldn't even get to the bathroom. But despite that, I had one hope: That it would only be six days more of agony, and then hopefully it would leave me alone, and it did. After that I went to another neurologist, and he recommended Sumatriptans, but otherwise mantained the same useless oxygen prescription. A year later in 2019 it came back again, same ol' story, and for the first two days I tried the Sumatriptan pills, which again did nothing, but as I was recently diagnosed with T2 Diabetes, I had a lot of syringes at home, so I bought Sumatriptan vials and started to apply it myself, starting with small doses and adjusted, until I came to the conclusion that 4mg was enough. I'd apply it and smoke enough weed to down an elephant, and I'd get a fast relief and fall asleep soon after, although I'd feel like shit the next day, but hey, it was just for a week and it was lightyears better than going through that agony over and over again, plus I thought that banging my head against the wall couldn't be good for my brain.

Dear Clusterheads,
The time has come to introduce myself to our beloved clusterheads family.
I was observing You for long time, but now I feel obliged to give my input.
My name is Radoslaw and I'm from Poland, suffering for CH since 2016 (maybe 2015, I don't remember properly). First two years without proper diagnose, but with small 1-2 weeks episodes during autumn each year. In 2018 I had 4 weeks terrible headaches, so I started my journey through neurologists. With help form the web knowledge I made a diagnose which was later confirmed by doctors - cluster headaches. Firstly doctors proposed me verapamil, but after cardiac research they refused to give it to me because of bradicardia ( a lot of wrestling and boxing in the teenage years made my hearth rate about 50-55). I didn't want to take lithium and gabapentin because of side effects ( no other therapy was known here in PL)
In 2019 in June my cluster appeared once again, stronger than before, so I desperately tried to find new solution. I found new doctor ( who isn't neurologist but is a specialist in headaches - brilliant guy !). Together we decided to take a risk and start with verap 480, under direct control of electro-cardio-diagrams. BOOM ! worked perfectly. My hearth rate felt down to 45-50 ( during night even to 40) but I felt good, without side effects, so I decided to continue it. For next 2 years I even forgot that I have this terrible disease ( for my bad...). Unfortunately I didn't stop taking verap after cluster period, but stayed on it for the whole year. In 2021 each attempt to reduce verap caused some shadows and single attacks, so my doctor suspected that I went to chronic version ( today I'm wondering that maybe I'm not chronic, but such long period of verap caused attacks when I tried to stop it - slowly as recommended).
In 2022 in June ( when I was on 480 verap) cluster hit with double power. I increased verap to 960, but it was very risky. It took 4 weeks to stop the cluster but I got also atrial block 1 stage at the end of cycle. I felt really dizzy, so 960 is little too much for me. Since that time, when I was on 480 some single attacks appeared during the year.
Today I'm 34 and I'm really concerned about using verapamil with bradicardia for such a long time. I have concerns wbout hearth pacemaker in the future, so for that reason I decided month ago to stop with verap and to go with regime D and busting with witamin L.
I know the oxygen method, I have 3 tanks at home with proper flowmeter and 1 small tank that I have always in my car. This is my first choise for attack. I'm using also a lot of caffeine in energy drinks.
To be honest I had some journeys with witamin L in my early 20s, so I wasn't worried about psychedelic trip ( maybe it was even good reason to try it once again).
On 8th of April I started regime D ( initial D level was 22, so very low).

First CH's started November of 2015.  Thought they were a result of a hangover, caffeine withdrawals, or dehydration.  True cycles started to emerge in spring 2016.  Misdiagnosed by ER as Migraine.  Correctly diagnosed as CH later.  Went through the cycle of preventative and abortives.  Benefits were often limited, side effects were intolerable.  Methodically tried elimination diets, med shifts, etc.  Most with some degree of correlation, but none with lasting relief.  Latest preventative was visiting a local chiropractor.  He noted in Chiropractic school, a member of his cohort was a CH suffer and they focused a bit of Chiropractic intervention with success. I gave it a try.  (Will add notes at the bottom on the impact of other meds)
When engaging chiropractor, I chose to not disclose much beyond CH.  He ran an x-ray panel and review with me.  In his review, he commented that the misalignment of C1 to C3, and other issues in the cervical vertebrae and other markers would lead him to believe I had suffered whiplash ~10 years earlier.  I had not disclosed in Dec of 2002, I had a head-on collision that I chose not to pursue medical treatment for.  He proceeded with a 3-month cycle of adjustments, 3x weekly, with a focus on addressing cervical issues.
In the 3 month cycle, which started about 2 weeks after the start of my CH cycle, the headaches stopped almost entirely.  3 months in, I have a few breaks through CHs,  average 1 or fewer per day (which is miraculous as on cycle I am consistently at 3x daily, on a schedule [2:31 am, 6:00 am, and 2:00 pm]).  I've noted that, when I miss the Chiro adjustments (due to work, etc) greater than 4 days, there is a higher potential to suffer more than 1 CH.
As we all know, CH triggers and resolution is mostly about correlation, as causality is not yet well established.  But thus far, chiropractic adjustments, coupled with stretches, shoulder and neck muscle exercises focusing on extension / relaxation, with heat on neck seem to have demonstrated the most effective treatment path for prevention without side effects.
Hope it helps. I am in Beaverton Oregon and see a chiro here.  I can provide a referral if interested.

This is the first time I have posted here.   I have been visiting the clusterbusters site off an on for the past couple of years - but I have never felt compelled to post anything here until now.  Every time I read a post where someone describes what they are going through I can relate.  Some of the stories here are really sad - and I hope these people take the busters' advice because its all good and has helped me immensely.  I have been reduced to tears reading many of the stories because I know what it is like, and I have had help and I am finally making progress and I there might be some light at the end of this very dark tunnel I have been living in for years,
I have been dealing with CH now for 13 years.  For the first couple of years I was told that I had a a severe case of Sinusitis and that was what was causing my pain.  The doc would give me some Avamys or some other nasal cortosoid, some antibiotics and send me on my way.  This 'worked' for a while as my clusters only lasted for a couple of  weeks and I figured that the sinus infection had passed and I was fine again.  This happened two to three times a year.  No biggie.   The attacks became worse and less tolerable as time went on.   I started to suspect that I did not have sinusitis - even though I felt like I had been kicked in the face and my sinuses were plugged.  At the time I did not have a regular doctor and if I showed up at the hospital or urgent care facility suffering from a CH I was often dismissed as 'drug seeking' and sent home, because the time that any doctor decided to see me the CH had passed and I was feeling 'normal' again.  I did eventually find a doctor who diagnosed me with CH, but he did not know too much about it and treated it like a standard migraine.   Maxalt as an abortive and amytripaline as a preventative - that didn't work, but at least I had a name for what I was dealing with now.  10 years ago the internet was not what it is now.  Cluster Headaches were less known and there were virtually no options for people.  My CH was episodic for 8 years - then they starting getting worse.  The cycles got longer and more frequent, until I got to the point where I am now.   Chronic CH - 4-6 times a day for the past  five years.  I get my 'breaks' from time to time where I will experience a one or two days CH free and I have good days (1 or 2) and I have really bad days (6+ attacks per day).
About three years ago I was fortunate enough to be put in touch with the CHAMP (Calgary Headache Assessment and Management Program) in Calgary.   The team of docs there are nothing short of amazing.  I have had MRI's done, I got a prescription for oxygen (a bit of a struggle to get initially from GP - CHAMP said NO PROBLEM), I got a scrip of Sumitriptan (Immitrex) and they started trying out different preventative meds.   I tried Verapimil.   It didn't really help me, I kept going for my ECG's and all was well until I hit the effective dosage and then BAM the worst tension head

Hey all. I’m new user of the forum and of the Clusterbusters site, although I’m fairly certain in the past I read through the countless resources available here. Anyhow I guess I’ll get on with my story and questions.
My story is likely similar to many others. I’m a male in my early 40’s and have been suffering from episodic cluster headaches for around ten or so years that I can remember. My episodes always occur in the Spring, always around the Spring time change, and typically every other year. My episodes begin with a single evening attack that develops over time into multiple attacks over a 24 hour period. They have a range of 3-5 months and usually consist of 8 insanely painful attacks with an ever present shadow pain that goes on even past my episodic end.  Unfortunately last year was an off year for me and my return to the nightmare started on March 21 (Cluster Headache Awareness Day). Yay me. I seriously dread life and dread speaking about this, as I know where time is going to take me and it infuriates and saddens me immensely.
In the early years prior to my diagnosis I had all but lost my will to live, having lived with a constant indescribable pain for which doctors offered zero assistance and medicines offered no relief.  I had visited countless doctors and neurologists who conducted countless tests and scans. It wasn’t until my second bout with episodes that I was diagnosed with CH, which was largely based on my own research. The neurologist prescribed several medications that failed to work. Hardcore pain meds, lithium, blood pressure meds, oxygen, oral triptans, etc. At that time I recalled reading that many ch sufferers were having luck aborting using triptan injections and I requested a prescription of my own. To my surprise the injection worked, although with their own odd side effects (made me feel as though I was being choked or short of breath and a slight burning discomfort). That first time was a miracle and I quickly fell asleep, having been living as an exhausted zombie for months, and slept for several hours for the first time in months. That first time held off attacks for nearly 24 hours before I again had to inject again. And again and again. My first script was for six injections so back to the doctor I went. As it turns out he wrote a script for more but insurance only would permit six injections a month! Are you kidding?! I have six to eight attacks a day and am being told I can only get six treatments a month? Anyhow I fought with the insurance company and they ultimately doubled what they would partially cover bringing me up to twelve single doses. I ended up only using the injection during the nighttime attacks, typically the attacks that wake you during sleep, and the massive attacks in the evening if I just couldn’t take it. I forced myself to endure the other daytime attacks and stockpiled everything I could on off months. This continued and I relied on the injections as my form of relief since then.

Hi everyone!
I've had cluster headaches for over a decade now and have finally gotten them to a place where they're manageable. Here's my story!
I first started getting cluster headaches when I was in 3rd grade, around the same time I started puberty, which I know is young. For a while doctors told me they were due to stress, TMJ, or that they were psychosomatic. I asked my mother if I could see a neurologist but she didn't believe the pain was severe enough to warrant it - which was incredibly demoralizing to me as a little kid.
Fast forward to high school. My headaches had been getting worse and worse and I was getting them more frequently as well. I was deeply suicidal and depressed. I begged my parents to send me to a psychiatrist. Finally, they did. The first one I saw had me on a bunch of different pills, none of which helped. He diagnosed me with ADHD and generalized anxiety disorder. My mom grew frustrated with him and took me to see another psychiatrist, who this time diagnosed me with bipolar disorder. He put me on lithium - it didn't really help with my sadness, but it helped with my headaches very rapidly!
I was overjoyed. I did some googling - at the time I thought I might have migraines - and discovered that lithium is effective for cluster headaches, not migraines. I brought this info to my psychiatrist, asking if he could refer me to a neurologist. He said my headaches were psychosomatic and that I should just calm down. After a few months of being on lithium, my thyroid started to be affected. He took me off lithium and switched me to lamictal. After the switch, I had the worst headache I had ever had in my life. I told my mom to drive me to the emergency room or else I was going to kill myself, the pain was so intense. They put me into psychiatric inpatient and titrated me back up on lithium.
I continued on lithium for a few more years, taking meds to help w the side effects. After I went to college, I started dating someone who wanted to try LSD with me. I figured out I should stop the lithium before doing this, so I went off it, and took a pretty high dose of LSD. After the LSD, I stayed off lithium for a year and had no headaches during that time. I had moved from the northeast to san francisco, so I thought the cause might be the more temperate climate - cold had always triggered my headaches before. Unfortunately, I started getting headaches once more. I did some more research, discovered that psychedelics were often a treatment for CH!
I made an appointment to see a neurologist, both to talk with her about the LSD and get a prescription for oxygen. At the time, I still believed I was bipolar, so I was concerned about taking a psychedelic. Now I'm convinced my mental anguish was from being in an extreme amount of pain and not being believed. I havenÂ’t wanted to post on this because as we all know the beast is unpredictable, and I do not want to scare anyone off of using triptans because they obviously work in terms of getting rid of the pain, and it just may be I am going chronic.

I usually get an hour at most lying down before waking in agony, and that continues as long as I am trying to sleep. O2 aborts fine, and I would stay clear as long as I donÂ’t try to go back to sleep. Basically, if I donÂ’t want to get hit, I donÂ’t even lay down. My pre-triptan sleep cocktail evolved from trying to pry that hour window open to 5 or 6 hours to get sleep. But that cocktail was pretty heavy, taking a few aborts in the morning before returning to the usual 10 and 2 hits but otherwise clear day. So, I started taking amerge (naratriptan), then switched to relpax (eletriptan) for sleep and getting 6-8 hours pain freeÂ… but at what cost I am wondering.

Hi to everybody!
First thanks to all of you and the forum. I appreciate very much your efforts in fighting the`Beast`...
I am a EC since about 20 Years, having my first Cluster Attack after a root extraction of a tooth. My Episodes have been reliably precise of one Attack late at night for 2 months, followed by a remission period of also 2 months. For about the firs 14 years. Then I camew across a Neurologist, who had treated me with Verapamil and Sumatriptan. The first shot of Sumatriptan was so amazing and wonderful. There was finally something, that worked on the Attack. Verapamil has aborted about 3 or four cycles. It had completely no effects on the following cycles except massive sideeffects.
From there, it has totally messed up my reliable pattern, and episodes got longer, (remission periods also).
During the last full year, i had not a remission period at all, except under Prednisone, starting with 100mg/day, reducing to 0 within 3 weeks.
as soon as the Prednisone is out of my body, the Pain starts again.
During this year, the frequency ranges between 2 and 8 Attacks/day. (that means also 2 to 8 Sumatriptan injections/day) So i´m now chronic.
I have been on Oxygen, Naratriptan, Prednisone, Lithium as well as numerous `unconventional` treatment attempts like Headachepowder obtained from a Maassai-Healer, inhaling burning Elephant-Dung, Spiritual Healer, electrified acupuncture needles, Regimes of Vitamines,
up to six cans of Red Bull/ day, Ocziptal-Nerve blockage, Gammacore and Tens-stimulation.
Marihuana doesn´t work either. It just makes the time in between the Attacks more pleasant....
2 years ago, i tried Sclerotia with no succes. But as I learned now, that it may not work while in Triptans, i wanted to give it another try under abstinence of the injektions. Then i kept using Sumatriptan injections very frequently.
3 weeks ago, i started with aprox. 3g of fresh Sclerotia (as 10g is a recommended recreational dose).
2nd dose after 3 days, 3rd dose after 5 days and a fourth after 5 days, but this time 1.6g of dried Psilocybe cubensis.
each time the psychoactive experience was very similar although not entirely pleasant, as i believe, that this amount gets me in between the two worlds, as many users experience.
anyhow, after the first dose, i had an attack the next morning, but on Level 3-4 (usually attacks are ranging on 7-9 on my personal painscale of 0 to 10) and lasting only 10-15 minutes. No injections needed.
Some days no Attack at all, usually one relatively mild Attack. But they do get stronger and last longer every day, until i took the next dose.
The last dose i took the day before yesterday, no Attack until last night, pain level 1 for 6 minutes, and another one followed this morning of Level 4 for about 15 minutes
during the last 2 weeks i tried the SPUT- method on 3 Attacks, with relief of 2 of them.
So all in all I see a huge difference in frequency, but mostly severity of the Attacks. And I´m Injection-free since the first

Hello from Australia,
Not as familiar with the CH lingo and hopefully posting to the right area. But I do know the Beast well. Him and Me have had many discussions about his malign and evil presence. The pain has been something only a fellow sufferer could appreciate.
First started getting CH 35 yrs ago and effective treatment was non existent. It took 5 yrs to be diagnosed not by a Dr but by a nurse. In the beginning, attacks started off when I went to bed and after dealing with intense pain would go back to bed only to be woken some 25 min later with another one coming on. This would last all night. As I said treatment was non existent. The CH would commence at the turn of the seasons and last for up to 3 months (twice year) . Looking back I don't know how I managed the pain every night for so long. My attacks would last for 30-40 min each. I decide to log every one to see what triggers were occurring or some sort of pattern.
If I started to get headaches after drinking beer, (sometimes just a shot of liquor would do it) then I knew CH's were on their way. It filled me with a deep dread & a knowing of helplessness. Within 3-4 days they would be on me.
Have tried many treatments over the years, (panadol, Tramadol, some Peth, Oxycodone, Lithium, Verapamil etc etc) mostly to no avail. Our GP Dr's are not well versed in CH and to a large degree you are on your own. Medical knowledge is certainly not as mature as US and no clinics specialising in this area. Online communities sparse.
I have had the CH change sides twice. I also suffered migraines in between attacks with full aura.
Currently dealing with a bout that has gone for 29 days straight (4 attacks during day and 6/7 at night). The CH  are starting in the late afternoon, maybe this is what are called shadows and get progressively worse till eventually morphing into a full blown CH some 2-3 hrs later. These different to the night time ones which come on after going to bed. Some 25 min later after falling asleep I wake and CH goes ballistic within 5 min. Not a lot of time. Insomnia is a thing now and the CH have impacted me greatly. I feel my brain has been rewired due to CH and subsequent treatments. Not all of it positive.
Its very difficult if not impossible to get oxygen due to living in a nanny state where home use of oxygen is considered a safety hazard. Mostly I manage CH with starting a course of Prednisolone up to 60mg a day for 10 days. This seems futile when there is no treatment other than Immigran to fall back on.
When a CH starts I get very active doing very vigorous exercises, (this would give me 5-10 min grace) and then take an Immigran tablet, hoping it will cut to the chase before the pain goes ballistic. This works 2/3 out of 5 . I do have needles but I only use these when desperate or just before passing out. The needles cost $60 each and are not subsidised. Have found out that I suffer terribly with rebound headache's from the triptan and force myself to reduce the amount (

Hi

Hello Peeps,
This is a discussion that will certainly continue and evolve for many years.
On main point to remember and is talked about below in my explanation of the 5 day rule, is that we know some medications, including psilocybin, can have an effect on how the body reacts, for a period of time after it has all left the building. So half life isn't the only consideration. Example, psilocybin can have a drastic effect on the number of cluster attacks you'll have long after it has all left the system. The topic here regarding Imitrex also takes into consideration that it has an effect even after it's left the system. The 5 day rule takes these other issues into account, besides merely s a blocker.
The Imitrex issue revolves around this fact. After stopping the regular use of imitrex, there can be a very bad couple of days with increased attacks. (An effect after its left the body) After this initial effect of possible increased activity, this increase begin to decrease and usually by day 5 people are back to fewer attacks than they were getting while using Imitrex. I refer to this as reverting back to your baseline cluster activity. Yes there isn't a lot of published research on this but there is research that shpows that sumatriptan can increase  both the severity and frequency of cluster attacks.
With that I leave you with this more complete explanation of the 5 day waiting period.
Psychedelic treatment of cluster headaches
Protocol of 5 day waiting period explanation
Why the 5 day wash out period and time between doses?
When you are trying to set up guidelines that work best for the majority of people, many others will fall outside those boundaries. That doesn't make the guideline any less appropriate or true.
A lot of people ate a lot of mushrooms to determine some guidelines. ;-)
For best results, generally, 5 days is best. This was based upon a LOT of data collection a long time ago and continued monitoring in a less scientific way.
Busting with psilocybin or LSD works to stop the attacks much longer than the chemicals are in your system. How and why this is possible isn't known for sure although there are plenty of theories.
Why does waiting 5 days work best? Does it work best?
Yes and there are many reasons behind it. You try to account for these things when publishing "general rules" to go by.

text:
Hey guys... Clusterhead of five+ years... Here is my story, and hoping someone here can help.
I’m a intractive, bilateral, chronic sufferer for over five years. I was diagnosed fairly quickly... within the first two years. I have seen several neurologists, and more dr’s and nurses than I can count, in the ER. I’ve been on a laundry list of medications (including verapamil, lithium, imatrex injections/nasal spray, amnitriptaline, neurontin, xanax, indomethacyn, etc.) ... I have liquid O2, and have even tried the busting method twice... and have tried several street pharmaceuticals...
My Clusters seem to be getting worse, and unresponsive to many things that gave me SOME relief, in the past... (adderall/vyvanse, prednisone). The only thing that works (most of the time) is my O2. But not even that is 100%.
The beast has taken away who I am, who I wanted to be, thoughts of the future, and has had a devastating effect on my relationships. My lady is my ROCK... (a nurse, who has been a mama bear, in my corner.)  But, it has taken its toll on her as well.
The daily battle (5~8), almost every time I sleep (within 45min to 2hrs) has  reeked havoc, on my mind, outlook, temper, and the like.
I have had teeth pulled, sinus X-rays, laser holes in my eyes (to reduce pressure~ thinking it may be narrow angled glaucoma)

First, about me.Â  I am a male in my mid 50Â’s and have suffered from cluster headaches beginning in my late 20Â’s.Â  I am also an MD, but of course, I had to diagnose my cluster headaches myself.Â  Oxygen is an effective abortive for me.Â  I have tried ergotamine, verapamil, and lithium.Â  I have also used very short courses of steroids which knocks the headaches out for a few days while I get to therapeutic doses of preventatives.Â  Subcutaneous Imitrex injections of 2 to 3 mg. usually produces complete relief in a few minutes.Â  My clusters tend to be about 19 months apart and last 2 months.Â  Yep, IÂ’m a pretty typical episodic cluster headache person.
I first saw Dr. John Claude Krusz, PhD, MD somewhere around March 2007 at his clinic near Presbyterian hospital in Dallas.Â  He considered cluster headaches to be a medical emergency and made an immediate appointment for me.Â  I was in a severe cluster and was completely out of control.Â  He gave me an IV infusion of steroids and started me on Topamax.Â  For some reason, I had never tried this before.Â  Dr. Krusz really knows his medications.Â  His PhD is in pharmacology.Â  He got me started on a gradually escalating dose of Topomax as he tapered the steroids.Â  I got through the cluster better than previous ones as Topamax seems to work much better for me than lithium and verapamil.Â
The beast returned like clockwork in September 2008.Â  I returned to see Dr. Krusz during the third week of my cluster.Â  When I saw him, he told me that he had been using ketamine intravenously on many migraine headache patients with some success.Â  He stated that although anecdotal, the therapy seemed much more effective on cluster headaches.Â  There were no promises made.Â  He just said that it might possibly help.Â  I agreed to this therapy.Â  He and his nurse started an IV on me and slowly infused the ketamine over 1-2 hours.Â  Yeah, it sort of zonked me out, and my memories of the infusion are somewhat scrambled.Â  The nurse checked on me frequently.Â  After the infusion was over, I had no lingering side effects.Â  My wife drove me home, but I still felt perfectly fine.Â  He gave me a prescription for ketamine nose spray to keep a low level of ketamine in my system especially at night when the beast usually attacks.Â  Here is the note that I sent to him after my visit...

Phenylpropanolamine (PPA; Accutrim), also known as norephedrine and oxyamphetamine, is a psychoactive drug of the phenethylamine and amphetamine chemical classes which is used as a stimulant, decongestant, and anorectic agent.[1][2] It is commonly used in prescription and over-the-counter cough and cold preparations. In veterinary medicine, it is used to control urinary incontinence in dogs under trade names Propalin and Proin.
In the United States, PPA is no longer sold without a prescription due to a proposed increased risk of stroke in younger women. In Europe, however, it is still available by both prescription and over-the-counter. In Canada, it was withdrawn from the market on May 31, 2001.[2]

[edit]Pharmacology
Phenylpropanolamine acts as a potent and selective releasing agent of norepinephrine and epinephrine, or as a norepinephrine releasing agent (NRA). It also acts as a dopamine releasing agent (DRA) to a lesser extent. It works by mimicking the effects of endogenous catecholamines such as epinephrine and norepinephrine, and to a lesser degree dopamine.
Many sympathetic hormones and neurotransmitters are based on the phenethylamine skeleton, and function generally in "fight or flight" type responses- such as increasing heart rate, blood pressure, dilating the pupils, increased energy, drying of mucous membranes, increased sweating, and a significant number of additional effects.
[edit]Chemistry
There are four optical isomers of PPA: dextro- and levo- norephedrine, and dextro- and levo- norpseudoephedrine. d-Norpseudoephedrine is also known as cathine, and occurs naturally in Catha edulis ("Khat").[3]
Phenylpropanolamine, structurally, is in the phenethylamine family, consisting of a cyclic benzene or phenyl group, a two carbon ethyl moity, and a terminal nitrogen- hence the name phen-ethyl-amine. The methyl group on the alpha carbon (the first carbon before the nitrogen group), also makes this compound a member of the amphetamine family.

PLAYING WELL TOGETHER
Every doctor, every pharmacist, will warn about drug interactions...some chemicals simply do not play well with others in the body. Sometimes, one drug will block the actions of another. Sometimes, one drug will strengthen or change the actions of another, or the two together will cause something else entirely to happen.
The bad news for clusterheads: many medications commonly used to treat clusters will block the effectiveness of tryptamines. In fact, when the clusterbuster treatment doesn't work, most of the time it is because these drugs are interfering.
There are other drugs and substances that make the tryptamines work too well...that is, they enhance the psychedelic side effects of tryptamines, and make for some unwanted surprises.
Based on the reports of clusterheads and expectations based on research and theory, Clusterbusters made a list of drugs likely to interfere with the treatment. There are other drugs we are not sure will interact with tryptamines, and drugs that might enhance the psychoactive effect of tryptamines. And of course there are drugs and treatment that do play well with cluster-busting tryptamines.
Experience has shown the clusterbuster treatment works best when drugs that interfere are avoided for long enough to be cleared from the body. We call this period “detox,” short for “detoxification,” though technically, this is not the proper term. Many clusterheads use some of these drugs for other conditions, and should talk with their doctors before stopping or changing any medications; some cluster medications must be withdrawn from slowly with a prescribed schedule so, again, see your doctor first.
Dropping cluster meds can be difficult while the attacks keep coming, but there are ways to help you survive during detox. See COPING - BUSTER-FRIENDLY TREATMENTS:
https://clusterbusters.org/forums/topic/690-9-coping-buster-friendly-treatments/
and OXYGEN INBFORMATION:
https://clusterbusters.org/forums/topic/1433-10-oxygen-information/
SURE BLOCKERS
Triptans
Perhaps the most common culprits for ClusterBuster failure are the triptans, since they unfortunately are the most popular abortive treatments for cluster attacks. Sumatriptan (Imitrex) can stop an attack quickly, and it’s possible it foils the clusterbuster treatment more often than any other blocker. Other triptans are used less often, and there are fewer experiences, but for the sake of caution, these should also be avoided.
Sumatriptan (Imitrex®)
Zolmatriptan (Zomig®)
Rizatriptan (Maxalt®)
Naratriptan (Amerge®)
Frovatriptan (Frova®)
Almotriptan (Axert®)
Eletriptan (Relpax®)
Steroids
Steroids, the corticosteroids Prednisone and Prednisolone particularly, can be very effective at completely stopping cluster attacks while they are being used. They are also very effective at causing some nasty side effects, and most doctors prescribe them only for short periods in decreasing dosages.

In over 50 years of cluster headaches, Emgality is the only genuine relief that I have experienced. I didn't think true treatment would come about in my lifetime. Having emgality available to me is like finding Moses' staff. It is miraculous!
I am a 68 years old. I have chronic cluster headaches. I have had cluster headaches for more than 50 years, since I was 17. It was a few years before correct diagnosis. The clusters were episodic, until 10 years ago. Then, they became chronic. I have gone without high volume oxygen for, at least, one week, only once, since then.
Before Emgality, in my entire history with clusters, I have found significant relief from only two treatments, high volume oxygen and lithium.  I got smaller relief from ergotamine, lidocaine, melatonin and butorphanol.
The oxygen was easy peasy. It gave prompt, usually effective, relief with no side effects.
The lithium definitely eased the number, the frequency and the severity of the headaches, but only a little. The lithium relief came at a cost. It made me lethargic. It dulled my alertness and libido. I'm sure it contributed significantly to my weight gain and worst of all it enlarged my thyroid greatly on one side and, thus, I believe, it is the cause of my neuropathy. (I do not have diabetes.)
I believe that I have tried every treatment, most of them at least twice, including the recent Gammacore and mega vitamin d regimen, to no avail.
My expectations for Emgality were limited because the literature and the folklore was that it wasn't particularly effective on chronic clusters. It was effective, primarily, only on episodic clusters. Again, my clusters don't cluster. They are chronic.
In the first 2 months of Emagality treatment, using my oxygen consumption as a measure, the frequency of my headaches have been reduced by 82%. I feel like the severity has also been reduced by 80%. I may not be completely free of cluster prison, but I feel like I am on a very liberal parole.
Generally speaking, I am not a big pharma cheerleader. Personally, as a cluster headache sufferer, all I can say is thank God and Eli Lilly for Emgality!

During my last episode, 2 years ago, the Verapamil seemed to really work and O2 at home stopped anything that got through.
Well I think most people on this board have pretty much given up on Verapamil, a drug really not designed for CH sufferers (or any head ache), because of the side effects and/or its inefficiency to control CH.
It's probably difficult if not impossible to find a neuro who is "sold on busting stuff", and it would be surprising you could discuss any of it with him.
If you think the Verapamil works well and lets you function and live your life "normally" or so, you may want to consider keeping with that medication.
Personally, I was taking 720 mg Verapamil and this much of that lowered my pulse rate down to 50-53, which is almost unlivable... can't even walk, catching my breath after 3 steps, and the CH not still under control. So my neuro added 900 mg of lithium on top of it, and I had Zomig nasal spray (triptans) to kill any CH breaking through, and there were quite a few, even with that much drugs in my system.
I followed that Rx procedure 4 times I think. The first 27 years of my CH journey I had spent without a diagnosis and without any meds, relying mostly on Ice and coffee to handle the crisis, and some sort of a way relaxing myself difficult to explain simply.
On the other hand, if it ain't broke, don't fix it, right?Â
That's what my neuro was telling me to justify the fact she wasn't offering me any other treatment. But it was working...Â  according to her. She was urging me to use the Zomig more, but I hated the feeling of it... but she said there was not any side effects from Zomig, whis is ... well anyways, I was feeling so shitty with these meds, and still being attacked every other night with kip 5 to 7. And side effects included, on long term, possible heart problems, possible loss of my teeth (Verapamil),Â  possible permanent ear worms (lithium), heavy slap back head aches, and extension of the CH cycle in length of time...
Conclusion: if it ain't broken for you, why do you want to try to fix it? I'm really not sure mixing it with busting is the greatest idea. Yes it's more natural, yes it's better, probably cheaper, but for me busting is "the other treatment", the one neuros seem to try to avoid, and one that has you dealing more directly with what is probably the source of the problem: the hypothalamus, and one that is hardly compatible with any other medication.
Well that's my opinion, I wish other board users will come out and supply their own opinion to guide you towards an opinion of your own based on the largest scan possible of experiences from clusterbusters. I don't think you will find many to advocate Verapamil on this board, but you will at
www.clusterheadaches.com

Hi HCH...welcome to the family...good stuff above ^^^^
...thinkin' ya got enough to worry about without adding the chronic v episodic fear. It's gonna be what it's gonna be... and you can use what you learn here and elsewhere to deal with it...and you WILL! It was my biggest fear for years...spent a lot of angst energy on it...and then one cycle it happened. Turned out to be the same ol' shit in a different package ...even a little less intractable...all the same stuff worked better. YMMV... but worrying/fearing/dreading is distracting without benefit.....
….I am concerned with the shotgun approach though. Pred and verapamil and D3 and Lithium and MM/busting.  It is TOTALLY understandable and not unusual for many of us. BUT....the dilemma becomes...when 'something" works...WHICH ONE(s)!? You may suspect but you won't really know...then the whirly ride may REPEAT with the next cycle...
...good idea to stop the pred while the verapamil kicks in...any more pred after that risks some really nasty side effects. With this particular regimen, if unsuccessful the next step would be to begin increasing verapamil dosages to try and dial in the dose that works for you. Reported 'sweet spot" is 480 mg/day in divided doses of immediate release type. Some (like myself), sometimes go much higher (960-1040 mg/dy) depending on cycle (all these dosages much higher than blood pressure control needs...which is why you should have a headache specialist, they understand that). ECG's recommended to start with and after significant dosage increase..... as it
may
affect heart rhythm.  Barring other health issues or unmanageable side effects it's a pointless exercise to give up on a med without at least exploring dosage adjustment. I'm not endorsing or discouraging verapamil (worked for me but inconsistently) but I believe if you have a physician you trust, and it's a recognized treatment (like this), ya GOTTA give it a fair shot. Why lithium would be added puzzles me...... unless verapamil dosage change tried unsuccessfully...
….I third (4th 5th?) endorse the energy drink advice....that WITH OXYGEN saved my sanity, if not my life, taken/started immediately when a hit is sensed. Proper flow (at least 15 lpm), mask (non rebreather) and technique are CRITICAL. Don't give up too early on this combo....a bunch of clusterheads could testify that just the O2 itself is
LIFE CHANGING
...…..
Best
Jon
PS The D3 regimen has multiple benefits for ya whether you have CH or not....and MAY be ALL you need... I'd keep doing that no matter where you end up....
